author: @jackunger8634
text:
Elegant and brilliant build!

The conductive layers resistance could be measured and adjusted to a specified value prior to the second (electrical) insulator paint layer.

Very lightly sanding the thickness would increase resistance if too low.
Applying multiple thinner conductor layers might also work to get resistance to a specific value.

[An electrical engineering grad student  I met back in college would file a notch to adjust the value of carbon resisters to get the exact value  wanted and finish by sealing the cut with paint or fingernail polish.]

author: @JasonDrennen
text: Coming from a seasoned 3D printer guy. Well let's just say back when the DaVinci duo came out And yes it's junk but it got me into 3D printing. I'll tell you you don't have a bad idea there when it comes to using conductive ink. You don't want to go 5 mm bed. It's too much surface the heat now if you wanted to do two 3 mm beds and put conductive paint in the middle that might be worth looking into if you can get that ink nice and flat. you don't want to leave that extra metal hanging off. It's more weight than needs throwing around. You don't have to worry about it being perfectly flat just get a probe and it will self level your bed. Also them silicone heat pads are good if you buy a good one. My big printer has the PCB heater built on but I also have a high wattage silicon heat pad backing it up. Then I insulated the bottom of my bed so I can get to 150 c on my bed in around a minute. But I am curious about that conductive ink depending on how smooth you can get it you might be able to put it on top of a 3 mm plate then sit your glass directly on top of it. I'm not sure how good the heat with dissipate into the glass but the only way to find out is to do it.

author: @cabinfourus
text: I would imagine the size of the copper tape would make a difference. A 12-volt heated bed at 220mm square would need more amps than a 24-volt bed at the same size.  Most of the store-bought 3d printers use 12 or 24 volts and upgrading to 36 volts isn't really ideal, since one would have to purchase all new hardware to work with the 36-volt power supply needed.  You say it takes 30 minutes to heat up to 100 degrees c in the video, using 5mm copper tape. I am sure there would be a bit of math involved but one could shorten the heat time by using a different size copper tape with 24 volts.

replies.author: @beaumontlivingston8084
replies.text:
Or you could power the hotbed from an independent psu perhaps?  add a 12V or 24V switching relay to control the power going to the hotbed from the independent psu via the thermistor mounted on the hotbed. Whichever matches your system boards' system voltage.

I have a 1220mm (4ft.) heated bed that I made, runs on a dedicated 60V PSU (120V Mains), draws a bit of current but its not obscene (5.18~5.22A), pulls the heat from ART to 120C in a little more than a minute. I built it nearly the same as this procedure,  and the cost to build a 3D printer that's capable of doing objects up to 4ft cube was FAR less doing it as parts than it was to buy a  pre made or even a diy 220mm  with a bed that had a hard time reaching 100C.

author: @garyhoffmann1615
text: For a 3D printer bed that size 500 watt works well, however you don't have the option of variable voltage normally. Might be a good idea to test the heater before applying the last coat of insulating paint, then you can add more conductive ink to reduce the resistance to the achieve the desired result. For a bed that size you would need a 24V power supply and resistance of 1.15 ohms to get 500W. My 500W 300X300 printer bed takes around 5 minutes to get to operating temperature.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: then you spent money on it mate lol

replies.author: @garyhoffmann1615
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering There cheap on Aliexpress.

replies.author: @cabinfourus
replies.text: I thought I saw him change the voltage in this video from 12-24-36 volts. Wouldn't this be variable voltage? Are you talking about the mainboard voltage? I use a MOSFET with my setup and power it with 24 volts with a 310mm square bed. I am going to give this a try using this ink and copper tape but also build a separate one with silicone and Nichrome wire. I am only doing both due to my curiosity. I use something similar to Flex Seal to form around the Nichrome wire to make a flexible heat mat.  The Nichrome wire allows for variable voltage.

replies.author: @garyhoffmann1615
replies.text: @cabinfourus If your MOSFET is being controlled by a PWM signal then it is effectively variable voltage up to the maximum of your power supply voltage. So my previous comment is not correct in that regard. I use AC mains-powered silicon heaters anyway with the solid state relays. Using AC means you can use a much smaller DC power supply to run your printer as it does not power the heater. AC mains power with painted on material would be rather dangerous.

@Martin-bx1et yeah mirrors are quite flat, a good test is to look at a grid (like a notebook) through it.
Doesn't prevent tapering, but this can be fixed with adjustment screws.
The main problem with glass is shattering on heating.

I mean, it's probably flat enough for the purpose, but not as a surface plate, so I wouldn't call it 100% flat.

author: @scotttovey
text:
The idea of a paint booth being over kill for painting one or two of those is not very accurate. It in fact depends on the nozzle on the paint can.

A few months ago I painted a project and decided to do it in the bathroom so that the fan would evacuate the fumes. The nozzle on the paint can I used is designed to give you a broad spray which turned out to be a bad idea. 

I had a 20 inch by 27 inch bench covered with a shower curtain. Spray droplets were not only on the floor in back of me, but a good two feet on either side of the bench.

That broad spray, in addition to painting a wider path on the wood, blew over spray paint droplets all over the place. I literally had to use a scrubby to get the paint off the bathroom floor. Where a standard nozzle makes a mess in the area that you are painting, that broad spray nozzle tends to make the paint droplets fly all over the place. 
 
Just keep that in mind should you decide to use the new  fangled cans with a broad spray. It will be a very bad thing should the industry decide to discontinue the older, not so messy spray nozzles on those cans.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it also might be an idea not to paint stuff in the bathroom mate

replies.author: @scotttovey

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: on the temp and voltage, I have to ask, what is it laying on, maybe a lot of your heat is being lost to the table? I had trouble getting the unit I had on a prusa I have up to temp taking sometimes over an hour, and the upper temps it just would not reach period, then when printing started I would lose temp quickly causing issues, but it had zero insulation, it was just floating on springs and had a glass plate clamped to it, so I bought some ceramic fiber insulation and put two 1mm layers under it, using kapton tape to attach it to the bottom of the heater and ran a layer of that over the entire surface of the bottom so fibers were not exposed to the bottom to fall out of the printer, and after doing that it is now able to shoot up to the upper temps it could not reach before and even is so stable that I thought maybe I broke something but after testing it really is that it is super stable before printing and barely loses any stability while the bed is whipping back and forth (Y axis motion only there but still plenty to get air to circulate even if it is trapped as mine is, in a cabinet to keep the heat inside) so maybe if you got some sort of decent insulation under it, (2300F ceramic wool paper is what I think the search criteria is on ebay but if not, it is definitely in the section with kiln materials, and pro tip just buy a whole roll lol, I have because it was barely double what I would have paid for the roll I got of 2mm stuff for the giant 400x600mm heated printer bed I bought, so it did not seem to make sense to buy two sections like that when I could get a 2 foot by 25 foot roll for double the price, and since I have been using the extra for stuff like insulating the heater inside my hot melt glue gun, which now works so much better and gives continous feed of liquid glue instead of needing breaks to heat back up, and electric soldering iron's that now have no trouble getting hot and staying hot enough for even stubborn solder with the upper temperature end for solder where any hotter and it becomes brazing alloy LOL) I am not even sure what all else I will end up using this stuff for yet, but I am sure I will find something, especially since I have a little kiln that I am going to start using in my research, along with my TV tube based vacuum chamber system too, (larger picture tube so I can fit a small 200x200mm xy system for 3D printing in plasma and vacuum deposition research XD )

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh for sure mate - it was just on the table - I tried it with ceramic wool and it performed much much better - but I kinda thought it was a cheat as most folks don't do that I think - but you are 100% right I think and thanks for taking the time to post - really helpful - cheers

author: @greenspider3664
text: Could this be used to make a flexable heating pad assuming you chnage the base material?

replies.author: @satibel
replies.text:
You could either 
- use a silicone sheet and paint on it
- cast some nichrome or steel wire in silicone.
You can use any kind of plastic instead of silicone if you don't go over it's melting temperature.
You should also check that the conductive paint is flexible too, if it isn't it will crack and break the connection.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
can you try out the automotive engine and exhaust paint, to see if it works with that, and does not damage it? (the paint I am talking about has to be baked on to complete the cure, and its a bit less resistant to handling than say epoxy enamel and what not that you have used so far, and is an actual ceramic coating of some sort, so I am kinda wondering about conductivity through it too, as well as how well it handles the heating elements you paint on, causing possible flexing between the coating and the substrate,  if not maybe once I get some conductive paint, I can give it a go, as I have two different kinds here and some primer made specifically for it, for those wondering, I will give name and brand below of the stuff I have on hand. 

VHT SP102 flat black and VHT SP100 flat gray primer are the two I have on hand, temps supposedly good for are 1300-2000ºF (704-1093ºC) also note if you are looking for it on a shelf, it is named "Flame proof^ as I think VHT has a lot of other paint products many which are not good to the higher temps this is, and probably don't require baking it at 250ºF (121ºC) then at400ºF(204ºC) and one last time at 600ºF(315ºC) all in 30 minute increments with a 30 minute cool down time, which I would love to know why, guessing it has something to do with annealing it on the metal due to the expansion and contraction of both the paint and the metal maybe? still just odd) anyway 3 times you have to cook it at various temps while cooling it twice between so it is involved if you go by their instructions, which I used it to coat my water pipe and pipe cap based pressure vessel to keep the chemicals I was working with from reacting with the metal in the pipe or any coating they had on it :)

replies.author: @satibel
replies.text:
I'd say if it holds to 800C it's probably a form of ceramic or glass which is what enamel is.
Looking at the can it says silica ceramic, so yeah it basically is glass powder/enamel in some sort of adhesive which probably burns out or evaporates when the paint is heated.
Note that I'm not sure it's how it actually works.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @satibel sounds like a possibility, I have been working on an aluminum oxide coating system but to try and coat something with a monolithic aerogel form of aluminum oxide, if I can achieve it, I will definitely be trying to do the same thing with zirconium oxide next :) of course the ultimate would be to manage an aerogel form of tantalum carbide to hit something over 3000ºC resistance, since hitting a full 3900 something C melting point before it collapses but still if 3000 could be reached as an aerogel level of insulation, man the implications for kiln work, :)

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering making sure it had pores would probably be best on that count, ie annodizing it with a lot of acid in the solution to get the largest pores and the thickest pore layer of these pores with the lowest voltage due to electron flow through the electrolyte in the pores to the aluminum at the bottom of them, so you have something really spongy to bond the paint to, as I am guessing it is thick enough that it wont sag down into pores even 20 or so nm in diameter though I think backing off the acidity and clearing the pores out, then re doing the work will coat the bottom of the pores too, just not sure if it would cause a thickening of the pore walls, which would only be useful I guess if it were to fill the pores in with aluminum nitride as I think it was shown to be far more thermally conductive than AlO3 is, but don't quote me on that, all I can say is it is sold as a thermal layer between something conductive and heatsinks made of conductive metal so there is an insulation layer for electrical conductivity, with out that pesky interference of being thermally insulating too,. as a safe alternative to barium oxide since that stuff is apparently highly carcinogenic lol. (the barium oxide being carcinogenic not the aluminum nitride)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @ThomasAndersonbsf that's very interesting mate cheers - I never thought about the nitride - something else to look at lol - awesome

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering when I was typing that I had wondered how to get aluminum nitride to form as I am guessing its not easy to get the salt to move from point to point by anodizing or electroplating type techniques then I had the thought of maybe using a replacement to the oxygen in water that is where the oxygen in aluminum oxide from anodizing solid aluminum, comes from, to a nitrogen molecule like maybe using liquid ammonia as the medium instead of liquid water, just not sure of the logistics of it yet, I have more thoughts on it but cluttering up here seems pointless so probably just do a video on the different ideas and then one on the tests and results, just would be nice to be able to make structures pf AlN for those nonconductive (electrically) heat sinks that have high thermal throughput and resistance, like it has, but even basic flat sheets are pretty pricy compared to even just basic AlO3 ceramic plates. or even complex structures like filters and tubes lol.

author: @GnuReligion
text: Interesting!  In the next revision, you can make multiple parallel strips to lower/control the resistance so it will work with lower voltage.  Maybe glue in a 100k thermistor and add underside insulation, as well ... like carbon felt or silicone.

replies.author: @satibel
replies.text:
Using like 2-3mm kapton or any kind of electrical tape might work you could also make a 110V or even 220V version by staggering the strips, making a zigzag pattern.
Though I think using more coats of ceramic might be needed to avoid capacitive coupling of the top plate.

author: @themeek351
text: How far of a span can your ink dissipate heat through, before needing another electrode?  Can your ink work on a flexible steel sheet print bed?  My printer runs its heated bed currently at 12V, so I would need to upgrade my power supply!

replies.author: @solarhope
replies.text: Painting more coats would lower the resistance, and increase the power on the same voltage.
